hero-title: What even is this thing people call AI?
hero-lede: You have probably heard the word AI a lot lately, and it can sound like magic, or like something out of a film. Here it means something more down to earth: a piece of software that has looked at a huge number of examples, found the patterns in them, and uses those patterns to guess what comes next — a sentence, a picture, an answer. It is not a person, and it is not alive. What it is, is very good at guessing. On this page we'll look at what that means, where it helps, and where it trips up — and you can decide for yourself how much to make of it.

You've heard the word everywhere, and it can sound like magic. Here it means something plainer: a piece of software that has looked at a huge number of examples, found the patterns in them, and uses those patterns to guess what comes next — a sentence, a picture, an answer. Not a person, not alive — just very good at guessing. We'll look at where that helps and where it trips up, and you can decide what to make of it.

You've heard the word everywhere; here is what it points to. AI, in the way we mean it, is software that has learned patterns from an enormous number of examples and uses them to predict or generate things — words, pictures, answers. Not a person, not alive: very good guessing. We'll look at where that helps and where it slips, and leave the judging to you.

You've heard it everywhere; here's the referent. AI, as we mean it, is software that learned patterns from a vast number of examples and uses them to predict or generate things — words, pictures, answers. Not a person, not alive: very good guessing. Where it helps, where it slips, below — you do the judging.

The word is everywhere; here is the referent. AI is software that learned patterns from vast examples and uses them to predict or generate — words, pictures, answers. Not a mind: skilled guessing. Where it helps, where it slips, below — you judge.
what-it-is: Picture something that has read and looked at a truly enormous pile of writing and pictures — more than any one person could get through in a lifetime. It didn't memorise it all. Instead it picked up the patterns: which words tend to follow which, what a cat usually looks like, how an answer is usually shaped. When you ask it something, it uses those patterns to guess what fits — one small piece at a time. That is the whole trick. It doesn't know things or understand them the way you do, and there is no one in there thinking. It is pattern-matching, done extremely well.

Picture something that has read and looked at a truly enormous pile of writing and pictures — more than a person could get through in a lifetime. It didn't memorise it; it picked up the patterns: which words tend to follow which, what a cat usually looks like, how an answer is usually shaped. Ask it something and it uses those patterns to guess what fits, one piece at a time. That's the whole trick. It doesn't know or understand the way you do, and no one is in there thinking — it's pattern-matching, done extremely well.

Think of something that has read and seen an enormous amount — far more than any person could — and pulled the patterns out of it: which words tend to follow which, what a cat tends to look like, how an answer is usually shaped. Ask it something and it uses those patterns to guess what fits, piece by piece. That's the trick. It doesn't know or understand the way you do, and no one is in there thinking — it's pattern-matching, done extremely well.

Picture something that has read and seen an enormous amount — far more than a person could — and pulled the patterns from it: which words follow which, what a cat looks like, how an answer is shaped. Ask it something; it guesses what fits, piece by piece. That's the trick. It doesn't know or understand the way you do, and no one's inside thinking — pattern-matching, done extremely well.

It has ingested an enormous amount and extracted the patterns — which words follow which, what a cat looks like, how an answer is shaped. Ask it something; it guesses what fits, piece by piece. No knowing, no understanding, no one inside thinking — pattern-matching, done extremely well.
strength-and-trap: Being a brilliant guesser is its strength. Ask it to reword a clumsy email, write a first draft, shorten a long article, explain an idea in plainer words, throw out ten ideas, or turn a sentence into another language — these are all about finding what fits, and that is exactly what it's built for. But notice the catch hiding inside the same skill. A guesser guesses even when it's wrong, and it doesn't sound any less sure when it is. The smooth, confident wording stays the same whether the thing it just said is true or completely made up. So the very thing that makes it useful is the thing to keep an eye on. (There's a fuller map of what it's good at if you'd like one.)

Being a brilliant guesser is its strength. Reword a clumsy email, write a first draft, shorten a long article, explain an idea more plainly, throw out ten ideas, turn a sentence into another language — these are all about finding what fits, which is exactly what it's built for. But the catch hides in the same skill: a guesser guesses even when it's wrong, and sounds no less sure. The smooth wording reads the same whether what it said is true or made up. So the thing that makes it useful is also the thing to keep an eye on. (There's a fuller map of what it's good at if you'd like one.)

Being a brilliant guesser is the strength. Wording, drafting, summarising, explaining, brainstorming, translating — these are all about finding what fits, which is exactly what it does. But the same skill carries a catch: a guesser guesses even when it's wrong, and sounds no less sure. The confident wording reads the same whether what it said is true or invented. So the thing that makes it useful is also the thing to watch. (A fuller map of what it's good at, if you want one.)

Being a brilliant guesser is the strength — wording, drafting, summarising, explaining, brainstorming, translating are all about finding what fits. The same skill carries the catch: it guesses even when wrong, and sounds no less sure. Confident wording reads the same whether true or invented. So what makes it useful is also what to watch. (A fuller map of what it's good at, if you want one.)

The guessing is the strength: wording, drafting, summarising, explaining, brainstorming, translating — all finding what fits. Same skill, same catch — it guesses even when wrong, and sounds no less sure; confident prose reads identically whether true or invented. What makes it useful is what you watch. (Fuller map.)
where-it-slips: Knowing this, you can roughly guess where it slips. Hard facts and sources are a soft spot: because it's filling in what looks right, it can hand you a name, a date, or a citation that simply isn't real. Careful step-by-step maths can wobble for the same reason. So can very recent events, which may have happened after the examples it learned from. And it can't know anything private to you — your inbox, your situation, what you actually meant — unless you tell it. None of this means don't use it; it means it's a tool, and you stay the one who decides how far to trust each answer. Where would you check it before leaning on what it said?

None of this means don't use it; it means it's a tool, and you stay the one who decides how far to trust each answer. Where would you check it before leaning on it?

Knowing that, you can guess where it slips. Hard facts and sources are a soft spot — filling in what looks right, it can invent a name, date, or citation. Careful step-by-step maths can wobble for the same reason, as can very recent events that fall after the examples it learned from. And it can't know what's private to you — your inbox, your situation, what you meant — unless you say so. None of that means avoid it; it means it's a tool, and you stay the one deciding how far to trust each answer. Where would you check it first?

Knowing that, you can guess where it slips. Hard facts and sources are a soft spot — filling in what looks right, it can invent a name, date, or citation. Careful step-by-step maths can wobble similarly, as can very recent events past the examples it learned from. And it can't know what's private to you — your inbox, your situation, what you meant — unless you say so. Not a reason to avoid it; a reason it's a tool, with you deciding how far to trust each answer. Where would you check first?

From that, where it slips follows: hard facts and citations (it can invent them), careful step-by-step maths, very recent events past its examples, and anything private to you it wasn't told. Not reasons to avoid it — reasons it stays a tool, with you deciding how far to trust each answer. Where would you verify first?
